game_name: Wizardry: Proving Grounds of the Mad Overlord

danger_signals.visual_form: Dungeon maze disappears, replaced by monster sprite/portrait
danger_signals.meaning: Active combat threat; party health at risk

danger_signals.visual_form: Text-based indicator of 'Darkness' on dungeon square; visual maze becomes obscured
danger_signals.meaning: Loss of navigation control; party disoriented and vulnerable to ambush

danger_signals.visual_form: Sudden location shift without warning; player appears in unknown dungeon coordinates
danger_signals.meaning: Potentially lethal spatial danger; risk of landing in solid stone or hostile area

danger_signals.visual_form: Deeper dungeon levels accessed via elevator/stairs; increased monster complexity in encounters
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating threat intensity; enemies grow progressively more dangerous per level

danger_signals.visual_form: Party status shows all members dead; game state locked unless bodies recovered
danger_signals.meaning: Irreversible failure state requiring new party or body recovery mission

safety_signals.visual_form: Text-based town menu screen; character creation and equipment management
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone outside dungeon; no combat encounters possible

safety_signals.visual_form: Character roster and alignment compatibility checks (Good/Evil separation rules)
safety_signals.meaning: Preparation state; party composition validated before dungeon entry

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple spell levels per class (up to 7 levels); healing spells accessible at higher levels
safety_signals.meaning: Mitigation resources available; party resilience increases with progression

safety_signals.visual_form: Controlled fast-travel mechanic requiring coordinate input
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency over dungeon navigation; escape route from dangerous zones

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Good and Evil characters cannot share party; alignment affects NPC interactions and spell availability
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dungeon encounters filter enemy types based on party moral alignment

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Final boss encounter at dungeon level 10; visual representation as unique monster sprite
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Bottom dungeon level reserved as Werdna's domain; amulet artifact tied to his defeat
Town hub above dungeon; implicit safe haven governed by castle authority; dungeon is 'Proving Grounds' under this jurisdiction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: First-person dungeon maze view with party status display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Free movement through 20x20 dungeon grid; random encounters triggered by exploration

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dungeon maze hidden; monster portrait/sprite replaces maze view; combat menu interface appears
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turn-based combat engagement; party cannot move through dungeon until combat resolves

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character status changes from 'Alive' to 'Dead'; corpse remains in dungeon location
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dead character cannot participate in combat; body can be recovered by new party; loot remains in place

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All party member statuses shift to 'Dead'; game state freezes; cannot exit dungeon
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game progression halted; player must create new party or abandon save file

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dungeon level counter increments (1-10); visual complexity and enemy sprite quality increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Stronger monsters appear; spell requirements increase; treasure quality escalates

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character experience level display; visual representation of elite class unlock (Bishop, Samurai, Lord, Ninja)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: New spells become available; combat effectiveness increases; access to higher-level dungeon areas improves

environmental_storytelling.example: 20x20 maze grid per level with permanent traps (Darkness, Teleport); spatial design communicates dungeon as hostile, maze-like fortress requiring player cartography
10-level vertical dungeon under Trebor's castle; deeper levels are progressively more lethal; final level contains Werdna's chamber—spatial hierarchy conveys authority and power
Equipment must be purchased before dungeon entry; rare treasures found in dungeon; amulet is final objective—material economy communicates resource management and long-term goals
No mid-dungeon saves; total party kill is permanent unless bodies recovered; death remains visible in corpse locations—mechanical design conveys real consequences for failure
New adventurers can descend to recover dead party bodies and loot; establishes cyclical dungeon population and emergent narrative of adventuring legacies

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Visual sprite appears; combat initiation; 1-4 groups can attack simultaneously; behavior varies by monster type and dungeon level
Can move, cast spells, attack, use items; participates in turn-based combat; experience accumulates
Cannot act in combat; body remains in dungeon location; cannot be resurrected in field; requires new party to recover
Final encounter boss; defeating him completes game objective; amulet is held as quest artifact

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Located at each dungeon level exit
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Visual landmark; enables level progression; hidden within maze requiring full exploration to locate

notes: Wizardry NES (1987) communicates world-state almost entirely through text-based menus and simple line-graphics dungeon visualization. Danger is signaled by narrative events (monster encounter screen) rather than environmental visual design. Faction identity is minimal—the game features alignment-based party restrictions but no visual faction sigils or color coding. World-state changes manifest as mechanical state transitions (alive→dead, level_0→level_10) reflected in UI rather than dynamic environmental visual shifts. The game's primary manifestation layer is procedural/mechanical rather than aesthetic; player understanding derives from rules comprehension and learned spatial navigation patterns. Regional differences (class name changes: Priest→Cleric, Bishop→Wizard in US version) suggest cultural faction adaptation but not visual manifestation changes.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Wizardry NES article)
